---
subtitle: "From Technical Capability to Verifiable Development Impact"
abstract: |
  This chapter closes Part I by mapping technical capability clusters from Chapter 2 onto specific SDG indicators, and by returning to the institutional comparison opened in @sec-institutional-baseline.
keywords:
  - SDG alignment taxonomy
  - indicator mapping
  - knowledge-policy nexus
  - data provenance
---

# SDG Target Mapping {#sec-sdg-target-mapping}

Mapping a technology cluster to an SDG headline number is easy; mapping it to a specific, verifiable SDG *indicator* is where most "AI for good" claims break down. This chapter closes Part I by holding the Chapter 2 catalogue's outputs to that stricter standard, and by returning to the UNU/OECD/ADB knowledge-landscape comparison opened in @sec-institutional-baseline.

## Alignment Taxonomy
- Mapping technical capabilities to specific SDG indicators (e.g., Target 9.4, 12.4) rather than headline SDG numbers alone.

## Identifying Negative Externalities and Trade-offs
- Where "AI for good" claims and material/energy costs diverge.

## Comparative Knowledge Mapping: UNU, OECD, ADB
- Extends @sec-institutional-baseline into a knowledge-to-policy comparison across the three institutions.
- Framed as fostering open, collaborative innovation across multilateral agencies, not a ranking exercise.

## Data Governance and Provenance for SDG Claims
- Minimum provenance metadata required before a claim can be cited in downstream chapters (DPP in @sec-digital-product-passports; RegTech in @sec-regtech-defa-alignment).

::: {.callout-tip title="Bridge to Part II"}
Part II turns this evidence base into the design layer: the extended ITU canvases.
:::
